q: Gottman's research shows enduring couples keep detailed love maps — a constantly updated knowledge of each other's inner world.
who: Ask one curious question about each other's day

q: Partners who keep learning about each other resist the 'I already know you' trap.
who: Note something new in each other's inner world

q: Gottman found masters turn toward bids for attention about 86% of the time, versus 33% for couples who later separated.
who: Respond to small bids instead of half-ignoring

q: The first three minutes of a disagreement strongly predict how it ends.
Repair attempts are the single biggest difference between stable and unstable couples.
Closing on common ground prevents the unresolved residue that quietly accumulates into resentment over months.
who: End hard talks with one point of agreement

q: Reflecting proves you actually received your partner — the core of perceived responsiveness.
who: Reflect back what your partner said before replying

q: A small concrete action outperforms reassurance because it shows you were listening and you followed through.
who: Do one small thing your partner mentioned needing

q: Undivided attention is the cheapest and rarest gift you can give in a distracted age.
who: Put the phone down when your partner is talking

q: Small kept promises build a felt sense of safety far more than occasional grand gestures.
who: Do what you said you'd do

q: A partner who can be reached when needed becomes a secure base.
Quick repair teaches the relationship a vital lesson: bad moments are survivable.
who: Name and fix any small rupture same-day

q: Rituals create reliable points of contact that survive busy weeks.
Couples with a shared sense of direction report more meaning and more resilience under stress.
Studies link natural 'we'-talk to higher satisfaction and better conflict recovery.
The Arons' self-expansion research shows novel shared activity reignites the excitement that routine quietly erodes.
Bringing new ideas into the relationship keeps each of you interesting to the other — a steady defence against the slide into boredom.
Deliberate novelty resists hedonic adaptation — the slow numbing to good things we already have, including a good partner.
Specific gratitude — naming the actual act — makes a partner feel truly seen, and predicts higher commitment far more than a vague 'thanks'.
Naming effort that usually goes unseen directly counters the drift toward taking each other for granted — a top quiet killer of relationships.
Expressed admiration builds fondness — the buffer Gottman found protects couples through their hardest seasons.
who: Tell your partner one thing you admire

q: Stress spillover is well documented: tension carried in from outside gets discharged onto the nearest person.
Buffering couple time from outside pressure preserves the relationship's repair capacity — the reserve you draw on when things get hard.
Sharing a hard day builds intimacy; making a partner absorb it erodes it.
who: Don't dump work stress on each other

q: A hand on the shoulder is a small daily biological deposit of safety.
Sincere compliments sustain positive sentiment — the lens through which neutral or clumsy acts later get read kindly instead of harshly.
A roughly 20-second hug or 6-second kiss crosses the threshold where bonding chemistry actually engages.
People who see relationships as cultivated persist through rough patches; 'soulmate' believers quit sooner when reality gets difficult.
who: Treat friction as buildable, not fatal

q: Relationships are maintained by accumulated small choices, not sustained by feeling alone.
A charitable read of ambiguous behaviour breaks the negative-interpretation spiral in which everything a partner does gets read as a slight.
No coldness, no silence as punishment. We ask directly and kindly.
No unresolved hurt goes to sleep with us.
Small and early, spoken or written. No suffering alone overnight.
Fully accepted, history included. It never enters a disagreement.
Neither pressure nor retreat.
Phones away, fully present.
Unhurried, private, listening to each other's bodies.
We show up loudly for good news, not just faithfully for bad.
Gratitude unspoken doesn't count.
Review, adjust, keep building.
